Mental Health Data Analysis

Data Wrangling Final Project — Certificate IV in IT (Data Science and AI)

Student: Aleksandra Schick
Course: Certificate IV in IT (Data Science and AI) — TAFE
Unit: Data Wrangling with Python
Date: May 2026

Purpose & Research Questions

This project has three equally weighted reports and two core objectives:

Objective 1 — Technical: Demonstrate data wrangling competency through the application of Python-based data cleaning, transformation, imputation, functional programming, unsupervised machine learning, and visualisation techniques across two structurally different real-world datasets.

Objective 2 — Analytical: Explore mental health through two complementary lenses — and then discover hidden patterns within them:

“Among people who self-report mental health symptoms — how severe is depression and anxiety, and who seeks treatment?”
(Report 1 — Clinical Survey Data)

“Among people who reach crisis point — who presents to a Western Australian Emergency Department for mental health reasons, and what happens to them?”
(Report 2 — WA Emergency Department Data)

“What hidden patient profiles and axes of variation exist within the WA mental health ED data — and what do they reveal that hypothesis-driven analysis alone cannot?”
(Report 3 — Advanced Pattern Recognition)

Connecting insight: These two datasets cannot be merged — they measure different populations using different instruments in different countries. Their value lies precisely in that difference: Report 1 captures the subclinical self-reported end of the mental health spectrum; Report 2 captures the acute crisis end. Report 3 then uses unsupervised machine learning to discover structure within the crisis data that goes beyond predefined hypotheses. The gap between Report 1 and Report 2 represents the untreated middle — people who are unwell but reach neither a survey nor an emergency department.

Report Summaries & Key Findings

Report 1 — Clinical Survey Data

Dataset: Social Media & Mental Health Survey (Braghieri et al., 2022)
Records: 579 US college students | PHQ-9 & GAD-7 clinical scales

Key findings: - 68.4% of respondents show moderate-to-severe depression (PHQ-9 ≥ 10) - Only 27.8% of those with clinical symptoms are receiving treatment - The treatment gap is largest among students with the most severe symptoms - Social media use correlates with higher depression and anxiety scores

Report 2 — WA Emergency Department Data

Dataset: WA EDDC Synthetic Dataset | WA Department of Health, 2022
Records: 949,612 total ED presentations — 59,287 (6.2%) mental health

Key findings: - Aboriginal/TSI people are 5.4x overrepresented in mental health ED presentations (17.8% of MH ED vs 3.3% of WA population) - 30.8% of mental health presentations result in hospital admission - 23.2% involve drugs and/or alcohol — nearly double the emergency triage rate compared to non-substance presentations - Only 2.9% of mental health ED presentations are potentially avoidable — challenging the assumption that MH patients misuse emergency departments - 8.3% leave without receiving care (did not wait or left at own risk) — 2.5x the general ED rate

Report 3 — Advanced Pattern Recognition

Techniques: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) + Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF)
Input: 59,287 patients × 7 clinical features

Key findings: - PCA reveals variance is evenly distributed across 7 components — mental health ED presentations are genuinely multidimensional - PC1 captures the acuity vs avoidability axis; PC2 captures the age vs self-harm axis - NMF identifies 5 distinct patient profiles emerging from the data: - Profile 1 (1.4%) — High urgency, metro, non-substance - Profile 2 (5.1%) — Older patients, substance + admitted - Profile 3 (18.5%) — Young, lower acuity - Profile 4 (15.5%) — Substance involved, not admitted - Profile 5 (59.5%) — Low complexity baseline

Datasets

Dataset Type Records Source
Social Media & Mental Health (Braghieri et al., 2022) Real — validated survey 579 ICPSR — US college students
WA Synthetic EDDC Representative synthetic ~950,000 WA Department of Health, 2022

Two Kaggle datasets (Gaming and Mental Health and Social Media Impact on Teen Mental Health) were initially evaluated but replaced with the WA EDDC dataset, which offers greater analytical depth, local relevance, and institutional credibility.

Methodology

Technique Report 1 Report 2 Report 3
Missing value detection & imputation ✅ ✅ ❌
Data type conversion & encoding ✅ ✅ ✅
Outlier detection using IQR ✅ ✅ ❌
Functional programming: map(), filter(), reduce() ✅ ✅ ❌
Chunked loading for large datasets ❌ ✅ ❌
PHQ-9 / GAD-7 clinical scale analysis ✅ ❌ ❌
Visualisation: bar, box, violin, line, heatmap, pie ✅ ✅ ✅
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) ❌ ❌ ✅
Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) ❌ ❌ ✅

All analysis is conducted in Python using pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, and scikit-learn. Reports are rendered using Quarto and published via GitHub Pages.

Ethical Considerations

  • The Social Media & Mental Health dataset (Braghieri et al., 2022) is openly licensed via ICPSR and contains no personally identifiable information.
  • The WA EDDC dataset is a synthetic representative dataset produced by the WA Department of Health. It contains no real patient records.
  • Neither dataset contains personally identifiable information.
  • AI tools (Claude) were used as a thinking partner during project planning and code development. All analysis decisions, interpretations, and written content are the author’s own. AI tool use is disclosed in accordance with TAFE academic integrity guidelines.
